skill.name: api-design
skill.description: Design REST and GraphQL APIs with guidance for resources, methods, responses, pagination, authentication, versioning, and OpenAPI documentation.
skill.summary: Create consistent REST, GraphQL, and OpenAPI designs for backend services.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged markdown backticks, documentation links, and API terminology as possible command execution, network access, weak cryptography, and reconnaissance. Review found only documentation examples for REST, GraphQL, OpenAPI, authentication, and HTTP references. No executable code, network calls, prompt injection text, or malicious intent was found.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Markdown Backticks Treated as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged inline backticks and fenced examples as Ruby or shell execution. The referenced lines contain API paths, HTTP methods, JSON, YAML, and GraphQL examples in documentation, not executable commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is plain markdown documentation and API examples. I found no script file, interpreter directive, command invocation, or user input execution path.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Documentation URLs Treated as Network Activity
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged hardcoded URLs. The URLs are example OpenAPI server values and reference links to public API documentation resources, with no code that fetches or transmits data.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The links appear only in markdown and an OpenAPI example. There is no network-capable code or instruction to send secrets to those URLs.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Crypto Detector Matched API Terminology
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged weak cryptographic algorithm patterns on lines containing words such as design, REST, versioning, descriptions, or sample JWT text. These are documentation terms, not cryptographic operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched content is skill metadata and API design documentation. I found no hashing, encryption, signing, or cryptographic implementation code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Reconnaissance Detector Matched API Error Examples
low_findings.description: Static analysis flagged lines in validation error examples as system reconnaissance. The lines describe example API error messages and fields, not host discovery or environment inspection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The context is an API error response schema. I found no commands or instructions that collect system, network, process, or filesystem information.

content.value_statement: Teams need APIs that are consistent, documented, and easy to maintain. This skill guides REST, GraphQL, OpenAPI, versioning, errors, pagination, and authentication choices.
actual_capabilities: Guides resource naming, endpoint structure, and HTTP method selection for REST APIs.
Provides response code, request body, response body, and error format patterns.
Covers pagination, filtering, sorting, and field selection conventions.
Compares authentication options including JWT, OAuth 2.0, API keys, and sessions.
Shows how to draft OpenAPI 3.0 documentation for common resources.
Includes GraphQL type, query, and mutation design examples.
limitations: Does not generate production server code or database migrations by itself.
Does not validate a live API against an OpenAPI specification.
Does not replace security review for authentication, authorization, or rate limits.
Uses general examples that must be adapted to each domain and compliance need.

use_cases.title: Plan a New Backend API
use_cases.description: Define resources, endpoints, status codes, request formats, pagination, and authentication before implementation starts.

use_cases.title: Document an Existing Service
use_cases.description: Turn endpoint behavior into a clear OpenAPI outline with schemas, parameters, responses, and examples.

use_cases.title: Choose REST or GraphQL Patterns
use_cases.description: Compare REST endpoints and GraphQL schema shapes for product requirements with nested data and flexible queries.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic REST API Plan
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning for a small resource-based service.
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a REST API for [domain]. Include resources, endpoints, HTTP methods, status codes, and basic request and response shapes.

prompt_templates.title: Add Errors and Pagination
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate design for list endpoints and validation behavior.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refine this API design with pagination, filtering, sorting, validation errors, conflict responses, and consistent response naming.

prompt_templates.title: Draft OpenAPI Documentation
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced documentation for a service contract.
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an OpenAPI 3.0 outline for these endpoints. Include parameters, schemas, response descriptions, authentication, and versioning notes.

prompt_templates.title: Compare REST and GraphQL Designs
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced architecture review for complex client data needs.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare REST and GraphQL designs for this product workflow. Explain schema choices, endpoint tradeoffs, caching impact, and migration risks.

output_examples.input: Design a user management API for listing, creating, updating, and deleting users.
output: A resource model for users with list, detail, create, replace, partial update, and delete operations.
Recommended success and error statuses for each operation.
Pagination, filtering, and sorting conventions for the list endpoint.

output_examples.input: Document an existing orders API for external partners.
output: An OpenAPI-oriented outline with order schemas, query parameters, request bodies, and response descriptions.
Authentication and versioning notes for partner integration.
A consistent error format for validation failures and missing resources.

output_examples.input: Decide whether a reporting feature should use REST or GraphQL.
output: A comparison of endpoint simplicity, query flexibility, caching behavior, and client needs.
A recommended API style with design risks and mitigation steps.

best_practices: Start with resources, relationships, and authorization requirements before naming endpoints.
Use consistent response shapes, error codes, pagination rules, and versioning from the first release.
Keep OpenAPI or GraphQL documentation aligned with implementation and tests.
anti_patterns: Do not mix verbs, nouns, and inconsistent path styles across related endpoints.
Do not return vague errors that hide validation details from legitimate API clients.
Do not choose GraphQL only for novelty when REST satisfies the client workflow.

faq.question: Can this skill design both REST and GraphQL APIs?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes REST endpoint patterns and GraphQL schema examples for queries and mutations.

faq.question: Does it create a complete OpenAPI file?
faq.answer: It can guide an OpenAPI 3.0 outline, but teams must complete domain schemas and implementation details.

faq.question: Can it choose authentication for my API?
faq.answer: It explains common options such as JWT, OAuth 2.0, API keys, and sessions. Final choice depends on your threat model.

faq.question: Is it suitable for public partner APIs?
faq.answer: Yes, especially for consistent documentation, versioning, pagination, and error handling conventions.

faq.question: Does it test API security?
faq.answer: No. It provides design guidance, but penetration testing and security review require separate tools and experts.

faq.question: Which AI tools can use this skill?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
